coredata.dc:title: Electron spin dynamics in sucrose-derived luminescent carbon dot-silica nanocomposites
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Physics and Chemistry of Solids
coredata.dc:creator: Savchenko, Dariya
Vasin, Andrii
Rusavsky, Andrii
Nazarov, Alexei
Lančok, Jan
Kalabukhova, Ekaterina
coredata.dc:description: In the present work, a bottom-up approach to synthesize luminescent carbon dot-silica (CDs@SiO2) nanocomposites from the sucrose precursor was utilized. The sucrose concentration and thermal treatment effect on electron spin dynamics in CDs@SiO2 nanocomposites was investigated by electron spin resonance (ESR) technique. A single ESR line resulting from exchange interaction between localized and non-localized electrons was observed. The non-localized electrons were related to carbon-related radicals in the sp 3-hybridized state, while the carbon dots (CDs) act as localized electrons. The observed narrowing of the exchange ESR line with temperature rise was explained by a combination of Anderson's spin-exchange motional narrowing and a hopping wave function. The high conductivity of the material is evidenced by the Dysonian lineshape of the ESR signal. The relation between CDs size and conductivity of the CDs@SiO2 nanocomposites was found. The metallic character of the CDs@SiO2 conductivity was explained by the quantum size effect that occurred due to the reaggregation of the CDs. It was concluded that the previously observed shift of photoluminescence (PL) band and further quenching of PL with the increase of sucrose concentration in CDs@SiO2 is caused by the quantum size effect.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Electron spin resonance
Exchange interaction
Nanocomposites
Spin dynamics

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Physics and Chemistry of Solids

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Ministry of Education and Sciences of Ukraine [Project F2211 ]; Operational Program Research , Development and Education financed by European Structural and Investment Funds and the Czech Ministry of Education , Youth and Sports [Project SOLID21 CZ.02.1.01/0.0/0.0/16_019/0000760 ]. E. Kalabukhova is grateful for support from the National Academy of Science of Ukraine [Project #23/21-Н ].

E. Kalabukhova is grateful for support from the National Academy of Science of Ukraine [Project #23/21-Н].

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The spin concentration of the paramagnetic centers estimated in CDs@SiO2 nanocomposites versus sucrose concentration for the samples annealed at different temperatures. T = 296 K.
Temperature dependence of ESR spectra measured in CDs@SiO2 nanocomposites prepared with different sucrose concentrations and annealing temperatures.

Experimental (black line) and simulated (red line) CESR spectra measured in CDs@SiO2 nanocomposites infiltrated with sucrose concentration of 800 mmol/l and annealed at 300 °C. T = 293 K (a), T = 20 K (b).

The temperature dependence of the total spin susceptibility estimated for CESR line in CDs@SiO2 nanocomposites infiltrated with sucrose concentration 400 mmol/l (a,b), 800 mmol/l (c,d), and annealed at 300 °C (a,c), 400 °C (b,d). The insets show the temperature dependence of the reciprocal spin susceptibility. Dots show experimental data, solid red lines are the Curie-Weiss law, and the blue lines represent the results of fitting with Eq. (4).

The temperature variation of the resonance magnetic field position of the CESR line obtained in CDs@SiO2 nanocomposites infiltrated with sucrose concentration of 800 mmol/l and 400 mmol/l and annealed at 300 °C and 400 °C. Dots are experimental data, the solid lines – the fitting with Eq. (6).

The temperature variation of the CESR linewidth in CDs@SiO2 nanocomposites infiltrated with sucrose concentration of 800 mmol/l and 400 mmol/l annealed at 300°С and 400°С. Dots are experimental data, the solid lines – the fitting with Eq. (7).

The temperature variation of asymmetric ratio A/B, obtained by fitting of the experimental CESR spectrum by Dysonian lineshape in CDs@SiO2 nanocomposites infiltrated with sucrose concentration of 800 mmol/l and 400 mmol/l annealed at 300°С and 400°С.

The values of the Curie-Weiss constant and g-factors for localized and non-localized electrons obtained by fitting the experimental data with Eqs. (4) and (6) in CDs@SiO2 nanocomposites infiltrated with sucrose concentration of 800–200 mmol/l annealed at 300°С and 400°С.

The values of the ΔB 0, A, T 2, τ, ΔE, n parameters obtained by fitting the experimental data with Eq. (7) in CDs@SiO2 nanocomposites infiltrated with sucrose concentration of 800–200 mmol/l and annealed at 300°С and 400°С.

head.ce:title: Electron spin dynamics in sucrose-derived luminescent carbon dot-silica nanocomposites

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Analysis of Functional Materials
Institute of Physics of the Czech Academy of Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present work, a bottom-up approach to synthesize luminescent carbon dot-silica (CDs@SiO2) nanocomposites from the sucrose precursor was utilized. The sucrose concentration and thermal treatment effect on electron spin dynamics in CDs@SiO2 nanocomposites was investigated by electron spin resonance (ESR) technique. A single ESR line resulting from exchange interaction between localized and non-localized electrons was observed. The non-localized electrons were related to carbon-related radicals in the sp 3-hybridized state, while the carbon dots (CDs) act as localized electrons. The observed narrowing of the exchange ESR line with temperature rise was explained by a combination of Anderson's spin-exchange motional narrowing and a hopping wave function. The high conductivity of the material is evidenced by the Dysonian lineshape of the ESR signal. The relation between CDs size and conductivity of the CDs@SiO2 nanocomposites was found. The metallic character of the CDs@SiO2 conductivity was explained by the quantum size effect that occurred due to the reaggregation of the CDs. It was concluded that the previously observed shift of photoluminescence (PL) band and further quenching of PL with the increase of sucrose concentration in CDs@SiO2 is caused by the quantum size effect.

ce:section.ce:para: The nanoparticles have found a wide application in the development of functional nanomaterials due to their unique physicochemical properties. The decrease of particle size to the nanometer range causes a significant change of their properties such as melting temperature, thermal capacity, electroconductivity, etc. Moreover, with the decrease in particle size, new optical, magnetic, and electronic properties were found in these materials. An increase in the surface-to-volume ratio causes the differences between bulk materials and nanoparticles with the same chemical composition. In contrast to the microparticles, the number of surface and subsurface atoms (∼ 1 nm), and grain boundary surface area, significantly grow in the nanoparticles. In addition, the tendency to aggregate is one of the crucial properties of nanoparticles.

Carbon nanostructures are novel and rapidly developing materials with significant potential for practical application thanks to the specific chemical and physical properties and capabilities for preliminary modifications and subsequent incorporation to the different matrix. A wide range of properties of carbon nanomaterials is determined by the ability of carbon atoms to form chemical bonds in the sp-, sp 2, sp 3-hybridized atom state in the different compounds.

Recently, carbon dots (CDs) attract a great interest in different technical areas and medicine due to their unique properties. The term CDs were introduced to specify the new type of carbon-based nanosized materials, consisting of discrete, nanosized quasi-spherical particles with d < 10 nm. Owing to their outstanding characteristics such as adjustable photoluminescence (PL), chemical inactivity, high degree of solubility in water, easy and low-cost production, and non-toxicity, CDs are widely used in the photo- and electrocatalysis, chemical sensors, biosensors, biointroscopy, and nanomedicine [1]. CDs have high oxygen content and graphitic and turbostratic carbon in different volumetric ratios [2]. CDs mainly contain sp 3/sp 2-hybridized carbon atoms and usually have an amorphous character. The main characteristic of the CDs is the strong PL, usually showing bright blue fluorescence with ultraviolet (UV) excitation, which depends on their size, wavelength of the exiting light, and functionalization of the surface.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are new zero-dimensional nanomaterials, having discrete quantized energy levels, the different density of states, and confined space over three directions. CQDs also attract special attention because of their essential properties such as tunable PL [3] enabling them to be used as effective universal fluorophores [4,5]. Specific electronic properties of CQDs are related mainly by quantum-mechanical effects, and such parameters as an energy gap, bandgap edge position, and the density of states, which are size-dependent in these carbon nanomaterials [6]. The reduction of the CQDs size modifies the density of states from a band structure to the discrete energy levels. With a decrease of the particle size up to the molecular/atomic size, the exciton becomes confined, and the energy bandgap structure turns into like that of a molecular electronic structure. The optical quantum-size effect appears when the nanostructure size approaches the Bohr radius, corresponding to the CQDs of about 2–10 nm diameters.

Fumed silica (SiO2) is one of the widely used matrixes for the incorporation of CDs. As a result, the obtained CDs@SiO2 nanocomposites possess unique PL properties with emission spectrum covering the broad spectrum from green to yellow region, which differs from blue fluorescence traditionally observed for CDs, and the wavelength of PL of the CDs@SiO2 nanocomposites is dependent of the excitation [7]. Moreover, the fumed silica is often used as a matrix for CDs promoting their dispersion and protecting the PL from quenching caused by an environment containing water and oxygen. In particular, CDs form covalent bonds with the SiO2 matrix, and, as a result, obtained nanocomposites possess good hydrophilic properties.

It is well known that electron spin resonance (ESR) is widely and effectively used as a non-destructive method for studying magnetic and electronic properties of defects and impurities in modern carbon-based nanomaterials, especially in materials containing carbon dots [8]. However, despite numerous ESR investigations of different carbon-containing nanomaterials, the properties of CDs were studied much less. For instance, the CDs in a strong alkaline solution showed an ESR signal at g = 2.0009, related to a singly occupied orbital in the ground-state CDs [9]. This singly occupied orbital means that CDs can be electron donors or acceptors upon the reaction. The increase of g-factor value for CDs from 2.0009 to 2.0022 was explained by a change in the singly occupied orbital in CDs after the reaction. At the same time, the CDs showed an ESR signal with a g-factor of 1.9903 related to a singly occupied orbital in ground-state CDs as well [10,11].

Our previous ESR study of the SiO2:C nanocomposites exhibits the EPR signal from carbon-related radicals (CRR) (g ∼ 2.0042–2.0035) with non-localized electrons trapped by CDs at the SiO2:C surface [12,13]. Also, it was found that annealing of SiO2:C nanocomposites leads to the emergence of oxygen in the vicinity of CRR and results in the spin density increase of the conduction CRR. It was established that the non-localized electrons of the CRR are in strong interaction with the localized spin system, which was attributed to the CDs with g ∼2.0002–1.9994. A correlation between the existence of the CDs and PL efficiency was found: a higher PL intensity was detected in SiO2:C nanocomposites containing a higher concentration of the CDs [12]. At the same time, the reduction of the PL was observed in SiO2:C nanocomposites annealed at high temperatures (T ann > 800 °C), which have a structure close to the graphitic. The structure variation of the carbon layer in SiO2:C nanocomposites with the rise of the annealing temperature up to T ann ≥ 700 °C was interpreted by the saturation of the carbon dangling bonds with hydrogen due to the formation of the CHn radicals, which released from broken methyl groups [13].

At the moment, all methods for obtaining the nanomaterials are divided into two main approaches, including bottom-up and top-down methods [14]. In the bottom-up methods, the assembling of nanostructures was carried out from the individual atoms and molecules. The top-down methods, in turn, are related to the decomposition of microparticles into nanoparticles. One of the bottom-up methods is the fabrication of the CDs on the base of carbohydrates. Carbohydrates are one of the major and important classes of biomolecules containing oxygen, hydrogen, and carbon. Carbohydrates as the precursors for the CDs synthesis are attractive due to their wide spreading, availability, heterogeneity, high solubility in the water, low carbonization temperature, low cost, and toxicity [15]. Therefore investigators recently started to use the advantages of carbohydrates to synthesize modern, highly fluorescent CDs with improved properties. For instance, simple monosaccharides including glucosamine, mannose, fructose and their derivatives, and ordinary disaccharides (sucrose, lactose, and maltose) were used to fabricate the highly fluorescent CDs by different methods [16–18]. Particular attention was focused on the fabrication of the CDs using sucrose [19–24]. It was shown that CDs fabricated from sucrose can be functionalized via organic dyes by amide bond formation with carboxylates, which cover CDs' surfaces. As a result, the fluorescence of the CDs was improved, and cytotoxicity was reduced [18]. The fabricated fluorescent CDs using sucrose as a carbon source showed an improved intensity of the PL and good dispersion in the water and were successfully used for bioimaging [20]. The CDs fabricated using sucrose as a carbon source also showed an intense PL and good biocompatibility, opening the perspective to use them in bioanalysis and biomarking [22]. Using sucrose as a carbon source allowed obtaining biocompatible CQDs exhibiting emission in the near-infrared range, which is very important and valuable for investigating the living organism tissues in medicine [24]. In addition, it was reported that sucrose-derived water-soluble CQDs have a deep UV emission of 4.1 eV and a PL quantum yield of 7–11% [25].

In the present study, the role of the CDs in the magnetic and electrical properties of the CDs@SiO2 nanocomposites prepared using different concentrations of sucrose water solution have been studied by the ESR technique. The single ESR line originating from the coupling of non-localized electrons provided by carbon-related radicals (CRR) and localized electrons related to CDs was found in CDs@SiO2 nanocomposites. The significant growth of the spin concentration of a single ESR line was observed with the increase of sucrose concentration and temperature annealing. The spin dynamics of the conduction electrons coupled to the localized electrons have been investigated in the wide temperature range by ESR in CDs@SiO2 nanocomposites. The ESR signal revealed temperature-dependent spin susceptibility, linewidth, resonance field position, and line asymmetry ratio. It was found that the lineshape of the ESR signal varies from almost symmetric Lorentzian to asymmetric Dysonian lineshape with the increase of the sucrose precursor concentration in CDs@SiO2. This effect was explained due to the quantum size phenomenon caused by the reaggregation of the CDs.

The CDs@SiO2 samples were prepared by infiltration of fumed silica (SiO2) matrix (S ∼ 295 m2/g, d ∼ 10–12 nm) by sucrose (C12H22O11) water solution as a carbon source. Different concentrations of sucrose solution (800 mmol/g, 400 mmol/g, 200 mmol/g, 8 mmol/g) and annealing temperatures (300 °C and 400 °C) were used to investigate the effect of carbon incorporation. The thermal annealing was carried out in nitrogen flow for 120 min. After thermal treatment, the samples were slightly darkened, indicating carbon clusterization.

The previously measured PL in these CDs@SiO2 nanocomposites [26] exhibited strong white light emission covering the spectral range between near UV and near-infrared region with maximum intensity at ∼450 nm. The annealing of CDs@SiO2 nanocomposites gives rise to the shift of the PL spectrum in the green-orange spectral range and decay of the integrated intensity of the PL spectrum with the growing carbon content, which was explained by the formation of the 3D carbon nanoclusters. Green-yellow emission revealed in CDs@SiO2 nanocomposites with high carbon content after thermal treatment was also related to the presence of 3D carbon clusters [26].

The ESR spectra were measured in a continuous-wave regime at the X-band frequency range (ν ∼ 9.4 GHz) on Bruker ELEXSYS E580 spectrometer equipped with ER 4122 SHQE SuperX High-Q cavity. The ESR spectra were investigated in the temperature interval from 5 K to 296 K utilizing ER 4112HV variable temperature helium-flow cryostat under the following conditions: microwave power level = 0.04743 mW, modulation amplitude = 0.1 mT, modulation frequency = 100 kHz, conversion time = 80 ms, and spectra resolution was 1024 points. The powdered nanocomposites were placed in the synthetic quartz tube with a diameter of 4 mm, and the 1,1-diphenyl-2-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) free radical with g = 2.0036 was utilized as a reference sample. We have used weak pitch reference sample from Bruker with the concentration of 1013 spins/cm to calibrate the spectrometer and enable the estimation of the spin concentration of paramagnetic centers in our samples.

The ESR lineshape was described by Dysonian lineshape that depends on the skin effect (the effect of the microwave (MW) amplitude decreases with penetration in the middle of the conducting sample) and the electron mobility [27]. The skin effect becomes apparent as the specific resistance of the sample is less than 104 Ω cm, i.e., when the sample has significantly high conductivity. In this case, the MW field becomes inhomogeneous over the whole sample volume and lower in the middle of the sample than that on the surface, reducing the sample volume interaction with the MW field [28]. The skin layer depth (δ) can be described with the following expression [29]:
(1)
δ =
1 /
π ν
μ r
μ 0
σ
,
where ν is the MW frequency, μ r ∼ 1 is the relative permeability, μ 0 is the permeability of free space, and σ is the conductivity.

The time, which is necessary for the penetration of the electrons through the skin layer T D, associated with the skin layer thickness δ, and can be described in the 3-D case as [30]:
(2)
T D
=
3
δ 2
/
2
ν e
Λ
,
where ν e is the average speed of the electrons, Λ is the electron mean free path. For the normal skin effect Λ ≪ δ.

For Dysonian lineshape, it is essential to know the relation between the T D time and Т Т time taken by an electron to pass through the whole sample. In the case when Т D/Т Т ≫ 1, the conductive sample can be regarded as wholly immersed in the perturbing radiofrequency field B 1. The effects of the diffusion or dispersion will not be observed because electrons do not enter and come out from the skin layer, which means that the electron is localized within B 1, and the skin layer is significantly larger than the electron path length. In this case, the symmetric Lorentzian lineshape is observed in the ESR spectrum.

When Т D/Т Т ≪ 1, the diffusion effect modifies the ESR line by admixture of the disperse component while the radiofrequency field is shielded (screened) by charge carriers in the conducting medium. Owing to field shielding, the shift between an electrical and magnetic component of B 1 causes the mixture of the dispersion and absorption components in the ESR signal. The relation between Т D and dephasing Т 2 (spin-spin relaxation) time becomes important in diffusion mode. If Т D/Т 2 → 0, the diffusion through the skin layer becomes extremely fast comparing to Т 2 time. It takes place in highly conductive metals, low temperatures, and/or very long Т 2 times. In this case, the ESR lineshape is mainly characterized by the dispersion component. The case when Т D/Т 2 ≫ 1 corresponds to the slow diffusing dipoles, intermediate or low conductivity when the paramagnetic impurities are distributed over the metal. The carbon-containing nanomaterials with relatively low conductivity than that in metals should be assigned to the latter case.

As a rule, classical Dyson theory is applied to the bulk conducting samples (crystals or films), while for the nanoparticles, it is expected that the symmetric Lorentzian lineshape to be observed because the particle size is less than the skin-layer thickness. However, the reaggregation of the microcrystals in nanoparticles may lead to the penetration of the electrons between electrically bound particles, and as a result, in the samples consisting of submicron particles, the spin packet may reveal Dysonian lineshape [31].

For spherical powder particle having diameter d, the CESR Dysonian lineshape can be described by the expression [32–34]:
(3)
d χ
d B
= A
− 2 x

x 2
)

+ D
1 −
x 2
(
1 +
x 2
)
2
,
where:
4 A
9

8
p 4

8

2
+ 1 ,
4 D

2

p =
d / δ
,
x =
( B −
B
r e s
)
/ Δ B
B is the magnetic field, D and A are dispersion and adsorption coefficients, correspondingly, B res is the resonance magnetic field position, ΔB is the linewidth.

The asymmetry ratio A/B between maximum and minimum of the first derivative absorption line is proportional to the sample conductivity can be obtained as [35]: A/B = 1 + 1.45D/A. When A/B = 1, the symmetric Lorentzian lineshape will be observed in the ESR spectrum.

The double integration of the Dyson-shaped ESR spectra was performed using the positive part of the derivative of the spectrum only in agreement with [36]. This procedure leads to the error in spin density determination not exceeding 15%.

The preliminary investigation of the sucrose infiltrated CDs@SiO2 nanocomposites before thermal annealing did not reveal any ESR signals. After thermal annealing, a single ESR line was detected in the samples with the sucrose concentration ≥8 mmol/g. The ESR signal intensity in the CDs@SiO2 samples prepared with a concentration lower than 8 mmol/g was beyond the detection limit of the ESR spectrometer due to the low spin concentration of paramagnetic centers (N S).

Fig. 1
shows the variation of the overall N S observed in CDs@SiO2 nanocomposites with different sucrose concentrations and annealing temperature (T аnn) measured at T = 296 K. As it follows from Fig. 1, the increase of the sucrose concentration leads to the growth of the spin concentration of paramagnetic centers. It was found that the increase of annealing temperature from 300 °C to 400 °C leads to the increase of the spin concentration in CDs@SiO2 nanocomposites by two times.

Fig. 2 shows the temperature variation of ESR spectra recorded in CDs@SiO2 nanocomposites prepared with sucrose concentration of 800 mmol/l and 400 mmol/l and T
ann = 300 °C and 400 °C. We do not represent the results of ESR measurements in the CDs@SiO2 nanocomposites prepared with sucrose concentration of 8 mmol/l and 200 mmol/l because the ESR spectra intensity was very low in those samples. From Fig. 2, it is seen that the ESR spectra represent a single line with isotropic g-factor, which was attributed to the paramagnetic center with S = 1/2. In addition, it was found that this single ESR line has temperature-dependent lineshape, intensity, resonance field position, and linewidth.

The lineshape of a single ESR line in CDs@SiO2 nanocomposites was found to vary with temperature from an almost symmetric shape at T = 296 K to a fully asymmetric one at low temperatures. This asymmetric Dysonian lineshape can be observed in carbon-containing materials when conduction electrons appear in the samples. Fig. 3
shows the example of CESR spectra simulation in CDs@SiO2 nanocomposites infiltrated with sucrose concentration of 800 mmol/l with T
ann = 300 °C by an asymmetric Dysonian lineshape using Eq. (3) at 293 K and 20 K.

Fig. 4 Represents the temperature variation of the CESR spectra integral intensity (proportional to the spin susceptibility, χ ESR), measured in CDs@SiO2 nanocomposites. The deviation from Curie-Weiss law at T > 100 K (inset in Fig. 4) can be explained by the temperature-independent Pauli-like contribution, occurring due to conduction electrons (the maximum contribution was observed in the CDs@SiO2 nanocomposites infiltrated with sucrose concentration of 800 mmol/l and T
ann = 300 °C, see Fig. 4c). As a result, the temperature dependence of the χ ESR can be described by the sum of Pauli-like term (χ p) and Curie-Weiss term (χ CW).
(4)
χ
E S R
T
=
χ p
+
χ
C W
=
χ p
+ C /
T - θ
where χ p = g 2 μ B 2 N(E F)/2, where N(E F) – density of states at the Fermi surface, g – spectroscopic splitting factor, μ B – Bohr magneton, C = Nμ 2/3k B – Curie constant, N – the amount of the paramagnetic atoms in 1 mol of the substance. μ – the magnetic moment of an atom, k B – Boltzmann constant, T – absolute temperature, θ – Curie-Weiss temperature (θ > 0 – the low-temperature spin alignment is ferromagnetic, θ < 0 – the low-temperature spin alignment is antiferromagnetic).

The fitting of Eq. (4) with experimental data shown in Fig. 4 allowed us to determine the θ values in CDs@SiO2 nanocomposites infiltrated with different sucrose concentrations and annealing temperatures. The obtained small negative values of the Curie-Weiss temperature (shown in Table 1 ) indicate that the weak antiferromagnetic coupling occurs in the localized spins system. With the increase of the sucrose concentration in CDs@SiO2 nanocomposites, the absolute value of the Curie-Weiss temperature becomes slightly higher.

Fig. 5 represents the temperature variation of the magnetic resonance field positions obtained by fitting the CESR spectra with asymmetric Dysonian lineshape for the CDs@SiO2 nanocomposites infiltrated with different sucrose concentrations and annealing temperatures. The resonance magnetic field values were normalized (calibrated) on the same MW frequency (9.4 GHz). As seen in Fig. 5, the resonance magnetic field values increase with the temperature decrease. Such temperature dependence for the resonance magnetic field position is typically observed when non-localized electrons (conduction electrons) are bounded with localized electron spins. The interaction of the non-localized and localized electrons gives rise to the exchange coupling between them.

The different impurities, which have unpaired spins or conduction electrons, localized for some reason, can provide the centers with localized electrons [37]. The dynamics of such systems can be studied using the Bloch-Hasegava equations for the magnetization of the localized and non-localized spins [38,39]. When the exchange interaction between subsystems exceeds the partial lattice energy (bonds), the correlation between oscillations of the spin magnetizations of the non-localized electrons and localized spins occurs, indicating that the relaxation process occurs in the bottleneck regime. In this case, the spin system is characterized by a single ESR line. In the opposite case, the ESR spectrum divides into two lines which correspond to each subsystem separately.

Thus, the observed temperature behavior of the resonance magnetic field position of the CESR line should be referred to the strong exchange coupling and can be expressed by the model of the two exchange coupled systems, including localized spins of the defects and non-localized electrons in the bottleneck regime [40]:
(5)
B
r e s
T
=
B
r e s , L S
⋅
χ
L S
∗
+
B
r e s , N S
⋅
χ
N S
∗
χ
L S
∗
+
χ
N S
∗
,
where χ LS* = χ CW(1 + a⋅χ P), χ NS* = χ CW(1 + a⋅χ P), a – the exchange interaction constant, χ CW and χ P – Curie-Weiss and Pauli paramagnetic susceptibilities for the spin systems of the localized and non-localized electrons, respectively; B res,LS and B res,NS are the resonance magnetic field positions for localized and non-localized electrons, correspondingly.

For the case when a⋅χ LS, a⋅χ NS ≪ Eq. (5) can be written as:
(6)
B
r e s
T
=
B
r e s , N S
+
B
r e s , L S
−
B
r e s , N S
⋅
C ∗
T − θ
1 +
C ∗
T − θ
,
where χ LS*/χ NS* = χ CW/χ P = C*/(T – θ) and C* = C/χ P is a temperature-independent value.

From the fitting of the experimental data shown in Fig. 5 with Eq. (6), the resonance magnetic field values and consequently the g-factors for localized (g LS) and non-localized (g NS) electrons in CDs@SiO2 nanocomposites were obtained taking into account the above-obtained θ values.

The g-factor values for localized and non-localized electrons are listed in Table 1. It was found that the g-factor value for the conduction electrons decreases with the increase of the annealing temperature. The resulting value for conduction electrons g = 2.0045(3) in the samples with T ann = 300°С was ascribed to the oxygen-centered carbon-related radical (CRR), while the value of g = 2.0037(3) in the samples with T ann = 400°С was related to the CRR with an oxygen atom nearby [12,13]. It means that the oxygen amount around CRR reduces while the spin concentration of the CRR increases with the enhancement of the annealing temperature.

The obtained g-factor values for the localized spins in investigated CDs@SiO2 nanocomposites are close to those previously cited in the literature for CDs [10,40]. Such g-factor values have been attributed to the CDs with orbital populated by one electron in the ground state, indicating that they may play a role of both donors and acceptors [41,42]. Therefore, we may conclude that in investigated CDs@SiO2 nanocomposites, the CDs play the role of the localized electrons.

Fig. 6 represents the temperature variation of the linewidth of the CESR signal obtained from the simulation with Dysonian lineshape in CDs@SiO2 nanocomposites infiltrated with different sucrose concentrations and annealing temperatures. The CESR linewidth in CDs@SiO2 nanocomposites at high temperatures is lower than 1 mT that enables us to conclude that carbon is in a sp 3-hybridized state in our samples [43–46].

The observed reduction of the CESR linewidth with temperature increase can be attributed to the motional narrowing effect, which appears due to the variation of the T 2 time with the change of their motional energy [47,48]. At high temperatures, spins begin to move faster, which allows them to spend less time near dephasing centers, and as a consequence, the coupling between investigated spins and dephasing centers decreases. This growing coupling leads to longer T 2 values, resulting in the narrowing of the ESR linewidth. On the contrary, at low temperatures, spins move more slowly, leading to the broadening of the CESR line.

Thus the observed temperature behavior of the CESR linewidth in CDs@SiO2 nanocomposites can be explained by the motional narrowing effect. Previously the motional narrowing effect for the ESR linewidth with Dysonian lineshape was observed in carbon nanotubes and theoretically was explained utilizing Anderson spin-exchange motional narrowing relationship in combination with hopping wave function [49–51]:
(7)
Δ B
( T )
= Δ
B 0
+
A
Δ E ⋅
[
1 + coth
(
Δ E
/
(
2
k B
T
)
)
]
,
where ΔB 0 = (ħ/g⋅μ B) (1/T 2) is asymptotic high-temperature (“metallic”) value of the ESR linewidth, A is a temperature-independent variable, T 2 = n⋅τ, τ is the average time each spin spend between locations, n is the number of hops up to the moment when phase coherence disappears, ΔE = ħ/τ is the energy required for the hops between two locations (energy activation).

The parameters ΔB 0, A, T 2, τ, ΔE, n derived from the fitting of experimental data from Fig. 6 using Eq. (7) are listed in Table 2 . From the analysis of the obtained parameters, we have found that spin dephasing time does not depend on the sucrose concentration and annealing temperature, while the activation energy value increases with the increase of the sucrose concentration. The highest n value and shortest τ time found in CDs@SiO2 nanocomposites infiltrated with sucrose concentration of 800 mmol/l and T
ann = 300°С enable us to conclude that these samples possess the highest conductivity.

Fig. 7 illustrates the temperature variation of asymmetric A/B ratio obtained by fitting of the experimental CESR spectra with Dysonian lineshape in CDs@SiO2 nanocomposites infiltrated with sucrose concentration of 800–200 mmol/l annealed at 300°С and 400°С. It is seen that the highest value of the A/B ratio revealed in CDs@SiO2 nanocomposites infiltrated with sucrose concentration of 800 mmol/l with T ann = 300°С, indicating that the conduction electrons concentration (electrical conductivity) has the highest value in these samples. Although the temperature variation of the CESR linewidth was explained well by the variable-range hopping motion effect, the temperature dependence of line asymmetry ratio was found to be not following the T −1/4 law.

It was expected that conductivity should decrease with decreasing the temperature as well as asymmetry ratio. However, as shown in Fig. 7, the temperature dependence of the A/B ratio shows an opposite behavior. We may suppose that this feature could be explicated by the temperature dependence of magnetic permeability [52]. Thus, the unusual temperature variation of the asymmetry ratio requires further detailed study and explanation. We suggest that the previously observed shift of the PL band and subsequent quenching of it in CDs@SiO2 nanocomposites with the increase of the sucrose concentration [26] is caused by a well-known quantum size effect [14]. It can be assumed that with the increase of the sucrose concentration, the size of CDs increases while the bandgap reduces, approaching the properties of CDs to the metallic, which is the reason for the appearance of the Dysonian lineshape in the ESR spectrum of CDs@SiO2 nanocomposites.

The role of CDs in magnetic and electrical properties of the sucrose infiltrated CDs@SiO2 nanocomposites has been determined by investigating the spin dynamics in strongly coupled electron systems associated with non-localized (conduction) electrons and localized paramagnetic centers by ESR. From the analysis of the exchange interaction between non-localized and localized paramagnetic centers in the bottleneck regime, the g-factor of the CDs was found to be g ∼1.9959(5)-2.0015(5). The observed exchange interaction between non-localized electrons and CDs is accompanied by a motional narrowing of the ESR linewidth in the range from 4.2 K to 50 K, in which the ESR signal integral intensity follows the Curie-Weiss law. The narrowing of ESR linewidth with temperature increase was described by the Anderson-Weiss spin-exchange narrowing model combined with the hopping motion of the electrons between CDs. The ESR signal can be related to unpaired electrons from carbon-related radical (CRR) with isotropic g-factor varying from 2.0045(3) to 2.0037(3) depending on the location of the oxygen in the vicinity of the CRR. The spin concentration of the paramagnetic centers in CDs@SiO2 nanocomposites grows with the increase of sucrose concentration. The ESR signal was described by Dysonian lineshape, indicating that CDs@SiO2 nanocomposites have high conductivity. The highest asymmetry ratio of the CESR lineshape was found in CDs@SiO2 nanocomposites with the sucrose concentration of 800 mmol/l and annealed at 300°С. It was suggested that the CDs size increases with the increase of the sucrose concentration, while the CDs band gap reduces, turning the conductivity of the CDs@SiO2 nanocomposites to metallic. Therefore, we may conclude that the previously observed shift of the PL band and further quenching of PL in CDs@SiO2 nanocomposites is caused by the quantum size effect.

Dariya Savchenko: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing - Original Draft, Visualization, Writing - Review & Editing. Andrii Vasin: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - Review & Editing. Andrii Rusavsky: Methodology, Writing - Review & Editing. Alexei Nazarov: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing - Review & Editing. Jan Lančok: Funding acquisition, Resources, Writing - Review & Editing, Project administration. Ekaterina Kalabukhova: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Writing - Original Draft, Writing - Review & Editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
